notable_gaps_and_anomalies: 2023 Dec: NO BOD — and March 2023 BOD was explicitly 'not happening' (file named so)
2024 April: Missing — no BOD file found
2025 Aug-Sep: Missing — two consecutive months without a BOD file
2026 Feb: Missing — no BOD in Feb 2026 (between Jan and Mar BODs)
Nov 2025 BOD labeled 'NovBOD no meeting' — BOD meeting cancelled outright
Jan 2025: 3 versions of JanBOD created — working, no-Guam variant, and final; suggests Guam operations were in/out of scope

deck_version_files.cadence_insight: Deck versions appear June 2024 and become weekly (or more) in H2 2024 through 2026. In 2025 there are 62 deck files versus 25 in 2024 — a 2.5x increase. This shift suggests the cash forecast is no longer just an internal treasury tool; it is being regularly exported into management/board presentations. The data-consumer base escalated materially in mid-2024.
deck_version_files.bi_weekly_cadence_start: Late 2024, ~weekly by January 2025 (Jan 3, Jan 7, Jan 14, Jan 21...)

hs_specific_files.insight: Home Services gets its first dedicated file tag in Oct 2023 (interco context). By May 2025, HS gets its own dedicated 'deck version' — meaning HS is being presented separately to leadership or external parties. The HS-specific cadence accelerates in late 2025 and continues into 2026, suggesting HS is either a strategic growth priority or a disposal/transaction candidate requiring separate tracking.

ad_prefix_files.interpretation: AD_ prefix appears on only 2 standalone files plus referenced in others (e.g., '_AD_' in HS version, 'adUPDATE' in Nov 2023 BOD, '5.28 deck backup_AD'). Likely 'AD' refers to a specific analyst or manager (possibly 'AD' initials). The AD_7.9.24 file and AD_6.17.25 file being standalone suggests this person creates their own branch versions — possibly an external advisor or senior stakeholder getting private copies.

post_reductions_march_2026.insight: Three versions of the March 3, 2026 file are all labeled 'post-reductions' — working, standard, and MarBOD. This is the single clearest event marker: a major cost reduction event occurred on or just before March 3, 2026. Three simultaneous versions (working, deck, BOD) means it was a board-level event requiring urgent scenario modeling.

payroll_events.insight: Supply chain payroll reduction of $2.8M flagged Aug 2025 for 2H26. Then 'pre payroll change' snapshot taken April 24, 2026 — day before another payroll change. The company is actively reducing headcount in multiple waves.

formula_instability.insight: Formula issues appear at key moments: Jan 2025 ('formula change'), May 2024 ('Aug formulas broken'), Aug 2023 ('Sep reformula'), Feb 2025 ('Logistics formula fix'). A working financial model breaking under operational complexity suggests the underlying business structure is changing faster than the model can accommodate.

new_format_jan2026.insight: January 2026 marks a 'new format' — coincides with the first 'asset sales' JanBOD file. The model was restructured at year-start 2026, likely to incorporate asset disposal proceeds as a line item, signaling a strategic shift toward asset monetization.

files: Daily Cash Fcst - 5.22.24_BU view_post online reduction.xlsx
online_reduction_may2024.insight: May 2024: post-online reduction file — the online/e-commerce BU was reduced (headcount, scope, or closed). This is consistent with Online disappearing as an inflows category between 2022 and 2026.

categories_in_2022_gone_by_2026: Auto Centers — eliminated (Sears Auto was wound down)
Sears Financial Services — eliminated (financial services arm closed)
new_categories_in_2026_not_in_2022: Online — added as explicit line item by 2026 (though then reduced in May 2024 event)
Note: 'Online' appears in 2026 headers but NOT in 2022 — reflects shift to e-commerce tracking
inflows_forecasting_comparison.insight: From 2022 to 2026, the revenue mix simplified dramatically. Three entire BUs vanished (Auto Centers, Monark, Sears Financial Services). Online was added explicitly. The surviving core is: retail stores (Sears/Kmart), Home Services, KCD brand licensing, and tenant/real estate income — a leaner, more asset-light profile.

legal_spend_analysis.fy26_budget_note: $16.3M budget vs $20.863M forecast — already $4.5M over budget at forecast level

firms_and_matters.note: Highest single matter spend; ~$1.7M also in 2025
Going to trial; settlement upwards of $1.2M

legal_spend_analysis.legal_risk_profile_insight: The legal spend portfolio reveals a company fighting on four simultaneous fronts: (1) HTS bankruptcy via Reed Smith — a subsidiary in formal insolvency proceeding; (2) LG lawsuit via Katten Muchin — likely a Korean-market IP or contract dispute around KCD/Kenmore appliances; (3) MOAC via Dechert+Wachtell — the Mall of America case is a major Real Estate dispute at the company's highest legal spend rate; (4) Landlord litigation wave across CA, MI across multiple firms — former Sears/Kmart locations generating sustained landlord disputes. Wachtell appearing across Corporate, SYW, and Real Estate simultaneously suggests they are the primary strategic counsel firm for the entire enterprise.
